core_behavior.note: Model A specifies detailed severity classification, propagation rules, and recovery mandates; Models B and C describe high-level detect-log-respond behavior without those specifics, reflecting independently introduced design choices on an undefined requirement.

inputs.note: Model A includes configuration errors and resource exhaustion signals not present in B or C; Model B includes security violations as an input type not listed by A or C; the requirement does not specify which error sources are in scope.

outputs.note: Model A specifies metrics increments, alerts, audit trail entries, and unique error codes as outputs; Models B and C omit these, with each model independently introducing different output artifacts.

assumptions.note: Model A has an empty assumptions array while Models B and C each list multiple assumptions including centralized handling, localization, severity categorization, and defined policies.

failure_modes.note: Model A has an empty failure_modes array while Models B and C specify behaviors for critical errors, logging system failures, and security-related errors.

edge_cases.note: Model A has an empty edge_cases array; Model B lists cascading failures and simultaneous errors; Model C specifies thread-safety and third-party library wrapping, each introducing different edge case concerns.

security_constraints.note: Model A requires audit trail entries for security-sensitive operations; Model B adds a DoS-via-logging concern; Model C adds secure log storage and regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR), none of which are specified by the requirement.

performance_constraints.note: Model A focuses solely on bounding retry loops; Model B addresses overhead minimization and high-volume handling; Model C specifies asynchronous handling, caching, and configurable thresholds, reflecting independently introduced design choices.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 5 of 8 fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement caused models to independently introduce incompatible design choices across most dimensions.
